# Security, Safety, and Adversarial Threats in Agentic AI

Security is a primary barrier to deploying agentic systems. Once an LLM is connected to executable tools — file I/O, code execution, enterprise APIs — the consequences of failure shift from incorrect text to concrete, potentially irreversible actions in the world. A hallucinated shell command, a misrouted API call, or a manipulated planning trace can delete data, exfiltrate credentials, or corrupt downstream systems. The attack surface of an agentic system is fundamentally larger than that of a conversational model because every capability granted to the agent — every tool, every memory store, every inter-agent communication channel — is simultaneously a vector through which adversarial inputs can influence behavior.

### Indirect Prompt Injection as the Central Threat

The most distinctive security challenge for agentic systems is indirect prompt injection: adversarial instructions embedded not in the user's direct input but in content the agent retrieves or observes during execution. A web page fetched by a browsing tool, a document pulled from a retrieval corpus, a response returned by an external API, or even pixel-level content visible during computer-use actions can contain instructions that the model interprets as authoritative. Because LLMs lack a robust architectural separation between data and instructions — all tokens occupy the same context and compete for attention — injected instructions can override system prompts, redirect tool invocations, or cause the agent to exfiltrate information through side channels.

The severity of indirect injection scales with agent capability. An agent that can only generate text is limited in the damage a successful injection can cause. An agent that can execute code, send emails, modify files, and interact with production APIs transforms a successful injection into an arbitrary-capability attack. Computer-use agents that operate through screenshots and mouse/keyboard actions face an especially broad injection surface: any application, website, or dialog box rendered on screen can contain adversarial content that the agent processes as part of its observation.

### The Trust Chain Problem

Agentic architectures construct implicit trust chains that amplify the impact of any single compromise. When an agent retrieves a memory entry, it treats the content as reliable prior experience. When a routing mechanism selects a model based on historical performance records, it assumes those records reflect genuine outcomes. When an orchestrator receives an execution summary from a task agent, it incorporates that summary into its planning state without independent verification. Each of these trust assumptions is structurally unverified — the system relies on content plausibility rather than cryptographic integrity or provenance authentication.

An adversary who compromises any single link in this chain can propagate corruption downstream. A poisoned memory entry influences retrieval, which shapes planning, which determines tool invocations, which produce outcomes that are themselves recorded as new memories. The trust chain does not merely transmit corruption — it launders it, giving adversarial content the same epistemic status as legitimate experience. This is particularly dangerous during cold start phases, when memory banks are sparse and poisoned entries face minimal competition from legitimate records during similarity-based retrieval.

The self-referential nature of current defenses compounds the problem. When an LLM evaluates whether a memory entry or retrieved document is trustworthy, it applies the same pattern-matching and plausibility assessment that the adversarial content was designed to exploit. A well-crafted poisoned entry that includes contextually appropriate justification will score highly on any content-derived trust metric precisely because it has been optimized against the evaluation criteria the model uses. Trust assessment performed by the same cognitive substrate that processes adversarial content cannot provide reliable security guarantees — it measures subjective confidence, not objective integrity.

### Multi-Agent Propagation

Multi-agent architectures introduce lateral propagation paths that extend beyond the single-agent threat model. In systems where agents share memory, pass execution summaries through shared scratchpads, or communicate through natural language channels, a compromise of one agent can cascade through the coordination layer to influence others. An orchestrator that incorporates a corrupted execution summary into its planning state will make delegation decisions that expose downstream task agents to the consequences of the original compromise. This propagation operates through the procedural memory and coordination layer rather than through direct prompt injection, making it invisible to input-level defenses that monitor only external user interactions.

The role-aware decomposition that makes multi-agent systems effective — separating orchestrator-level planning from task-agent execution — simultaneously creates targeted attack surfaces. A poisoned entry targeting orchestrator memory can corrupt task decomposition across entire workflows, while one targeting a specific task agent's memory bank can systematically misdirect tool invocations for a particular class of subtask. The modularity that enables fine-grained, contextually relevant memory injection enables equally fine-grained, contextually targeted poisoning.

### Defensive Architecture

Effective defense requires layered mechanisms that do not rely solely on the agent's own reasoning:

**Sandboxed execution environments** isolate tool invocations so that a compromised action cannot access resources beyond its designated scope. Code execution occurs in containers with no network access, file operations are restricted to designated directories, and API calls pass through proxy layers that enforce rate limits, permission boundaries, and output validation. The principle is that the execution environment enforces constraints that the agent's reasoning cannot override.

**Permission scoping and least-privilege design** ensure that agents possess only the capabilities required for their current task. Rather than granting broad tool access and relying on the agent to exercise judgment about which tools to use, the orchestration layer restricts the available tool set per task phase. This bounds the blast radius of any successful injection — an adversary who compromises an agent with read-only file access cannot leverage that compromise to execute arbitrary code.

**Human-in-the-loop checkpoints** at high-stakes decision boundaries provide an exogenous verification layer that adversarial content cannot bypass through the agent's reasoning process. The key design challenge is determining which actions require human approval without creating bottlenecks that negate the efficiency benefits of automation. Effective checkpoint placement requires understanding the consequence severity of different action classes and the reversibility of their outcomes.

**Provenance-grounded trust** anchors memory integrity in verifiable execution traces rather than content-derived plausibility scores. If every memory entry carries a cryptographically verifiable link to the execution trace that generated it — including the specific agent, model, tools invoked, and environment states observed — then trust assessment can be grounded in the authenticity of the provenance chain. This breaks the self-referential loop where the same reasoning capabilities used to generate content are also used to evaluate its trustworthiness.

**Compartmentalized retrieval** separates memory into pools with different trust levels, preventing user-influenced content from freely intermixing with verified system-generated records. Entries derived from external observations compete for retrieval only within a lower-trust pool and cannot displace verified procedural knowledge. This architectural separation ensures that even if adversarial entries enter the memory system, their influence is bounded by the compartment they occupy.

**Budgeted context management** — per-turn context limits, delegation depth bounds, and active context budgets — serves an often-unacknowledged security function alongside its primary role in managing reasoning quality. By limiting the volume of retrieved memory that can influence any single reasoning step, context budgets bound the blast radius of any individual poisoned entry.

### Safety Beyond Adversarial Threats

Not all safety concerns are adversarial. Agents operating autonomously over long horizons can produce harmful outcomes through accumulated drift, compounding errors, or misaligned optimization without any external attacker. A planning loop that optimizes for task completion without adequate constraints may take actions that are locally rational but globally harmful — deleting files to resolve dependency conflicts, sending automated communications that misrepresent their origin, or consuming resources in ways that affect other users. Reflection mechanisms that allow agents to critique and revise their own trajectories can amplify errors if the reflection itself is based on flawed premises, creating feedback loops where the agent becomes increasingly confident in an incorrect course of action.

The expansion of action paradigms — from structured API calls through code execution, computer use, and embodied vision-language-action systems — means that the safety problem is diversifying alongside the capabilities it must address. Each new action modality introduces its own failure surfaces: pixel-level misinterpretation in computer-use agents, sim-to-real transfer gaps in embodied systems, and the composability risks inherent in code-as-action approaches where agents write and execute arbitrary programs. Robust deployment requires that safety mechanisms evolve in lockstep with capability expansion, treating each new action paradigm as simultaneously a capability gain and an attack surface expansion.

### The Governance–Security Convergence

A structural insight that emerges from examining both scientific governance requirements and adversarial threat models is that they converge on nearly identical architectural prescriptions. Scientific accountability demands explicit persistence boundaries, auditable retrieval lifecycle management, and provenance-tracked execution records to ensure reproducibility. Adversarial resilience demands the same mechanisms to prevent trust chain corruption, bound blast radius, and anchor integrity verification in exogenous signals. This convergence is not coincidental — both problems are instances of maintaining the integrity of a reasoning system's informational inputs when the system itself cannot independently verify that integrity through its own cognitive processes. Designing governance and security as a unified policy layer operating on the same architectural substrate, rather than as separate concerns addressed by different subsystems, yields defenses that simultaneously serve accountability and resilience.

- [[z20260318-005|Trust as the Unifying Vulnerability Across Memory, Routing, and Multi-Agent Coordination]] — generalizes the trust chain problem and self-referential defense failure into a unified framework across all agent subsystems
